content.text:
{For God is not a God of confusion but of peace.}{1 Corinthians 14:33}
 {Order is the first requisite of any successful scientific undertaking.}{— Carl Friedrich Gauss}

When faced with a large system of equations, we need a systematic method 
to find solutions—or to determine that no solution exists. The method 
we develop in this section, called **Gaussian elimination**, is the 
foundational algorithm of linear algebra.

Instead of writing out variables and plus signs repeatedly, we organize 
the information from a linear system into a rectangular array of numbers 
called a **matrix**.

content.title: Augmented Matrix
content.content:
The **augmented matrix** of a linear system is 
the matrix whose rows contain the coefficients and constants from each 
equation, with a vertical line separating the coefficient matrix from 
the constant vector.

content.title: Writing an augmented matrix
content.problem:
Write the augmented matrix for the system:
$$
x - 2y + 3z &= 9 \\
2x + y - z &= 8 \\
3x - y + 2z &= 5
$$
content.solution:
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & -2 & 3 & 9 \\
2 & 1 & -1 & 8 \\
3 & -1 & 2 & 5
\end{array}\right]
\]
Each row represents one equation. The columns before the vertical bar 
represent the coefficients of $x$, $y$, and $z$ (in that order), and 
the column after the bar contains the constant terms.

content.text:
The key insight is that certain operations on the rows of a matrix 
correspond to operations on the equations that do not change the 
solution set.

content.title: Elementary Row Operations
content.content:
The following three operations on the rows of a matrix are called 
**elementary row operations**:

1. **(Replacement)** Replace one row by the sum of itself and 
 a multiple of another row: $R_i \to R_i + k R_j$.

2. **(Interchange)** Interchange two rows: $R_i \leftrightarrow R_j$.

3. **(Scaling)** Multiply all entries in a row by a nonzero 
 constant: $R_i \to k R_i$ where $k \neq 0$.

content.text:
Each operation is reversible, which guarantees that it preserves the 
solution set. Two matrices are called **row equivalent** 
if one can be obtained from the other by a sequence of elementary row 
operations.

content.title: Row Equivalence Preserves Solutions
content.content:
If the augmented matrices of two linear systems are row equivalent, 
then the two systems have the same solution set.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Our goal is to use row operations to transform any matrix into a 
standardized "staircase" pattern that makes the solution easy to read.

content.title: Echelon Form
content.content:
A matrix is in **echelon form** (or 
**row echelon form**) if:

1. All rows consisting entirely of zeros are at the bottom.

2. Each leading entry (leftmost nonzero entry) of a row is to the 
 right of the leading entry of the row above it.

3. All entries in a column below a leading entry are zeros.

A matrix is in **reduced echelon form** 
(or **reduced row echelon form**, RREF) if additionally:

1. Every leading entry is $1$.

2. Each leading $1$ is the only nonzero entry in its column.

content.title: Echelon forms
content.problem:
The following matrix is in echelon form but not reduced echelon form:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 3 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\]
The leading entries (2, 1, 5) are not all ones, and the first leading 
entry has nonzeros above it in its column.

This matrix is in reduced echelon form:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Each leading entry is 1, and each column containing a leading 1 has 
zeros everywhere else.

content.title: Uniqueness of RREF
content.content:
Every matrix is row equivalent to one and only one reduced echelon 
matrix.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem is powerful: it means that the reduced echelon form of a 
matrix is an intrinsic property, independent of which sequence of 
operations we use to find it.

content.title: Gaussian Elimination
content.content:
To reduce a matrix to echelon form:

1. Begin with the leftmost nonzero column. This is the **pivot column**.

2. Select a nonzero entry in the pivot column as the **pivot**. 
 If necessary, interchange rows to bring the pivot to the top.

3. Use row replacement operations to create zeros below the pivot.

4. Cover (or ignore) the row containing the pivot and all rows above it. 
 Repeat Steps 1–4 on the remaining submatrix.

5. Continue until no more nonzero rows remain.

To continue to reduced echelon form (**Gauss-Jordan elimination**):

1. Beginning with the rightmost pivot, use row operations to 
 create zeros above each pivot.

2. Scale each pivot to $1$.

content.title: Complete row reduction
content.solution:
Write the augmented matrix and row reduce:
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 5 \\
2 & 5 & 4 & 13 \\
1 & 3 & 3 & 8
\end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{R_2 - 2R_1}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 5 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
1 & 3 & 3 & 8
\end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{R_3 - R_1}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 5 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 3
\end{array}\right]
\]
\[
\xrightarrow{R_3 - R_2}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 5 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{R_1 - 2R_2}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 0 & -3 & -1 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]
\]
This is the reduced echelon form. The corresponding system is:
$$
x_1 - 3x_3 &= -1 \\
x_2 + 2x_3 &= 3
$$

Since $x_3$ is a **free variable** (no 
leading entry in its column), we can set $x_3 = t$ for any real 
number $t$, giving:
\[
x_1 = -1 + 3t, \quad x_2 = 3 - 2t, \quad x_3 = t
\]

The solution set is infinite, parameterized by $t \in \R$.

content.title: Pivots and Basic/Free Variables
content.content:
A **pivot position** in a matrix is a position 
that corresponds to a leading entry in the reduced echelon form. A 
**pivot column** is a column containing a pivot.

In a linear system:

- Variables corresponding to pivot columns are called 
 **basic variables**.

- Variables corresponding to non-pivot columns are called 
 **free variables**.

content.title: Identifying inconsistent systems
content.content:
A system is **inconsistent** if and only if its augmented matrix 
has a row of the form
\[
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & | & b \end{bmatrix}
\]
where $b \neq 0$. This corresponds to the equation $0 = b$, which is 
a contradiction.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss (1777--1855)
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen 
of the sciences, and number theory is the queen of mathematics.
